t: Order from smallest to largest: 1/2, 1/8, 1/4.
e: 1/8 is smallest (8 pieces = tiny). 1/4 is next (4 pieces = small). 1/2 is biggest (2 pieces = big).

t: Order from largest to smallest: 1/4, 1/2, 1/8.
e: 1/2 is biggest, then 1/4, then 1/8 is smallest. Fewer pieces = bigger slices.

t: Ava gets 3/8 of a pie. Ben gets 5/8 of the same pie. Who gets more?
e: Both fractions have 8 as the bottom number. 5 > 3, so 5/8 > 3/8. Ben gets more.

t: Which is larger: 2/4 or 1/2?
e: 2/4 and 1/2 are equal! 2 out of 4 parts is the same as 1 out of 2 parts.

t: A cake has 8 equal slices. You eat 4 slices. Did you eat more than half, less than half, or exactly half?
e: Half of 8 slices is 4 slices. You ate 4/8, which is exactly 1/2.

t: Jess eats 2/8 of a candy bar. Did she eat more or less than 1/4?
e: 1/4 = 2/8. So Jess ate exactly 1/4 of the candy bar.

t: Who ate more pizza: Maya who ate 3/4, or Eli who ate 6/8?
e: 3/4 = 6/8. They ate the same amount! Each fourth equals 2 eighths, and 3 x 2 = 6.

t: Which fraction is closest to 1 whole: 3/4, 1/2, or 1/8?
o: They are all the same distance
e: 3/4 is closest to 1 whole. It is only 1/4 away from a full whole.

t: A rope is cut into 8 equal pieces. You need more than half. What is the fewest pieces you need to take?
e: Half is 4/8. To get MORE than half, you need at least 5 pieces (5/8 > 4/8).

t: Kim has 1/4 of a pie. Lee has 1/4 of a different same-size pie. Together, how much pie do they have?
e: Kim has 1/4 and Lee has 1/4. Together that is 1/4 + 1/4 = 2/4 of a pie.

t: Noah says 7/8 is less than 1/2 because eighths are small. Is Noah correct?
o: No, 7/8 is more than 1/2 because 7 eighths is a lot
Yes, any fraction with 8 on the bottom is small
e: Noah is wrong. 1/2 = 4/8. Since 7/8 > 4/8, we know 7/8 is much MORE than 1/2.

t: Which is bigger: 3/8 or 1/2?
e: 1/2 = 4/8. Since 3/8 < 4/8, 1/2 is bigger than 3/8.

t: Two same-size pizzas are cut differently. Pizza A has 4 equal slices. Pizza B has 8 equal slices. Are 2 slices of Pizza A the same as 4 slices of Pizza B?
o: No, Pizza A slices are bigger
No, Pizza B slices are more
Yes, both equal 1/2 of a pizza
e: 2 slices of 4 = 2/4 = 1/2. 4 slices of 8 = 4/8 = 1/2. Yes, they are the same amount!

t: Which is closer to 0: 1/8 or 1/2?
e: 1/8 is closer to 0. It is a very tiny piece, much smaller than 1/2.

t: Put these fractions in order from least to greatest: 7/8, 3/8, 5/8.
e: All have 8 on the bottom, so compare the tops: 3 < 5 < 7. Order: 3/8, 5/8, 7/8.

t: Lina ate 1/2 of her lunch. Ravi ate 3/4 of the same size lunch. Who has more left?
e: Lina ate 1/2 so she has 1/2 left. Ravi ate 3/4 so he has 1/4 left. 1/2 > 1/4, so Lina has more left.

t: Is 4/8 greater than, less than, or equal to 2/4?
e: 4/8 = 1/2 and 2/4 = 1/2. They are equal!

t: A rectangle is split into 4 equal parts. 3 are shaded. Is more than half shaded?
o: No, less than half is shaded
Yes, more than half is shaded
e: Half of 4 parts is 2 parts. 3 parts are shaded, and 3 > 2, so yes, more than half is shaded.

t: Which is bigger: 2/4 or 3/8?
e: 2/4 = 4/8. Compare 4/8 to 3/8. Since 4 > 3, 2/4 is bigger than 3/8.

t: Finn has 5/8 of a candy bar. Sam has 3/4 of the same candy bar. Who has more?
e: 3/4 = 6/8. Compare 5/8 to 6/8. Since 6 > 5, Sam has more (6/8 > 5/8).
